era_context: The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648) dominates European politics; the Swedish Empire under Gustav II Adolf's successors remains a major power; the Qing Dynasty consolidates control over China following the fall of the Ming; the Ottoman Empire faces internal strain and external pressure from European powers; European colonial expansion accelerates in the Americas, Africa, and Asia.

events.title: Battle of Nördlingen
events.description: Imperial and Spanish forces under Ferdinand of Lorraine and the Cardinal-Infante defeated the Swedish and German Protestant armies under Bernard of Saxe-Weimar and Gustav Horn on 6 September 1634. This marked a major turning point in the Thirty Years' War, reversing Swedish military dominance in Germany and forcing France to enter the war directly in 1635. The battle resulted in heavy casualties and the temporary collapse of the Protestant military position in southern Germany.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts in Imperial and Swedish military records; Pufendorf's account in Historia Belli Suecici
Historiographic consensus in modern Thirty Years' War scholarship

events.certainty_note: Date and outcome are well-documented in contemporary military records and multiple independent sources.

events.title: Founding of Connecticut Colony (Treaty of Hartford)
events.description: English settlers under Thomas Hooker and others established the Connecticut Colony in the Connecticut River valley. The Treaty of Hartford (1638, but settlement began in 1634) formalized boundaries between Connecticut, Massachusetts, and the Pequot Nation. This represented expansion of English colonial presence in New England and displacement of indigenous populations.

sources.name: Colonial records and charters; Winthrop's journal

events.certainty_note: Settlement dates are well-documented in colonial records, though the formal treaty came later.

events.title: Pequot War escalation
events.description: Tensions between English colonists and the Pequot Nation escalated following the death of trader John Stone in 1633 and the killing of Captain John Oldham in 1634. English forces, allied with Narragansett and Mohegan peoples, launched military campaigns against the Pequot. This conflict would culminate in the Pequot War (1636–1638), one of the first major conflicts between English colonists and Native Americans.
events.certainty_note: Events are documented in colonial records, though indigenous perspectives are largely absent from surviving sources.

events.title: Founding of Maryland Colony
events.region: North America (Chesapeake Bay region, present-day Maryland)
events.description: Lord Baltimore (Cecilius Calvert) chartered the Province of Maryland, with the first settlers arriving in March 1634 aboard the Ark and the Dove. Maryland was established as a proprietary colony with religious toleration for Catholics and Protestants—unusual for the era. The colony's founding represented both English colonial expansion and an attempt at religious pluralism in North America.
key_figures: George Calvert, 1st Baron Baltimore (father, original charter holder)

sources.name: Maryland Charter (1632); colonial records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: Founding date and key figures are well-documented in colonial charters and records.

events.title: Qing Dynasty consolidation: Dorgon's regency
events.description: Following the death of the Chongde Emperor in September 1643 (but regency began earlier), the Qing Dynasty under the Regent Dorgon (uncle of the young Shunzhi Emperor) consolidated power. By 1634, the Qing had established the Later Jin state and were preparing for the conquest of Ming China, which would be completed by 1644. This period saw military reorganization and the integration of Han Chinese into the Qing administrative structure.

sources.name: Qing imperial records; Shiji and later Chinese chronicles

events.certainty_note: Qing administrative and military developments are well-documented in imperial records.

events.title: Tulip Mania peak (Dutch Golden Age)
events.description: The speculative bubble in tulip bulb trading reached its peak in 1634–1637, with rare variegated tulips commanding extraordinary prices. Traders bought and sold bulbs (often still in the ground) at inflated prices, creating a futures market. While the extent of the 'mania' has been debated by modern historians, it reflects the wealth and commercial sophistication of the Dutch Golden Age and the emergence of futures trading.

sources.name: Contemporary Dutch merchant records and correspondence
Modern historiographic reassessment (Goldgar, Dash) questioning the 'mania' narrative

events.certainty_note: The phenomenon is documented, but modern scholarship disputes the extent and severity of the 'mania' as portrayed in later accounts.

events.title: Banishment of Portuguese from Japan
events.description: The Tokugawa shogunate under Tokugawa Iemitsu completed the expulsion of Portuguese traders and missionaries from Japan, a process that had begun in the 1630s. This was part of the sakoku (closed country) policy that isolated Japan from most foreign contact. The expulsion reflected concerns about Christian missionary activity and foreign influence on Japanese society.

sources.name: Portuguese missionary accounts and later Japanese chronicles

events.certainty_note: The expulsion policy is well-documented in Tokugawa records and Portuguese missionary accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: The 'Tulip Mania' narrative has been significantly revised by modern historians (particularly Anne Goldgar). While tulip trading did occur and prices were high, the extent of the speculative bubble and the number of actual transactions at peak prices have been questioned. Contemporary sources suggest the phenomenon was less catastrophic than 19th-century accounts portrayed. Additionally, the Pequot War's causation is more complex than simple English aggression; indigenous political divisions and trade competition also played significant roles.
historiographic_note: 1634 is well-documented for European and colonial North American events, with rich primary sources from military campaigns, colonial charters, and merchant records. Asian sources (Qing, Japanese) are also substantial but often reflect official court perspectives. Indigenous American perspectives are largely absent from surviving written records, creating a significant bias toward European colonial narratives. The Dutch economic records are detailed but require careful interpretation to distinguish actual transactions from speculative offers.

graph_edges.note: Nördlingen's outcome forced France to enter the Thirty Years' War directly
Escalating tensions led to the formal Pequot War
Qing consolidation under Dorgon enabled the conquest of Ming China
